typival specs 1978

In 1978, computer specs were not standardized, and varied widely. However, a typical computer from that time had the following specs:

- Processor: 8-bit CPU such as the Intel 8080, Zilog Z80, or Motorolla 6800
- Clock speed: Typically around 1-2 MHz
- RAM: 4-32 KB (kilobytes) of memory
- Storage: Cassette tapes, floppy disks, or hard disks with capacities of up to a few hundred kilobytes
- Display: Typically a monochrome CRT or LED screen with resolution ranging from 80x24 to 256x192
- Input: A keyboard, with optional joystick or paddle controllers
- Operating system: Usually a simple proprietary OS, or sometimes a version of BASIC programming language.
giving a computer from 1980 4 mb of ram

It would not be possible to give a computer from 1980 4 MB of RAM as it was beyond the maximum capacity of computers back then. The most advanced computers at the time, such as the IBM PC in 1981, had a maximum memory capacity of 256 KB, and even that was considered a generous amount. It was not until the mid-1980s that computers started to have RAM capacities of 1 to 4 MB, and those were relatively high-end machines.
32 mb for windows 1.0 - 3.0

32 MB of RAM for Windows 1.0-3.0 would have been very excessive and unnecessary. When Windows 1.0 was released in 1985, the typical PC had only 256-512 KB of RAM, while Windows 3.0, released in 1990, recommended 1 MB of RAM for running basic applications like Microsoft Word. 

It wasn't until the mid-1990s when the software started to become more complex and demanding that computers required more than a few megabytes of RAM. Even then, by the late 1990s, when Windows 98 was released, the recommended RAM was only 16-24 MB. 

In summary, 32 MB of RAM was not necessary nor supported by Windows 1.0-3.0.
64 mb

Adding 64 MB of RAM to a system running Windows 1.0-3.0 would not make any significant difference in performance beyond the additional RAM that is already present in the system. These operating systems were designed to operate with much less RAM, and any excess RAM beyond the system requirements will not be effectively utilized by these operating systems. 

Moreover, computers that would have run Windows 1.0-3.0 were not built to support that much RAM. For example, Windows 3.0 released in 1990 recommended only 1 MB of RAM for running basic applications like Microsoft Word. So adding 64 MB of RAM to a system that old is beyond its physical limitations and would not provide any real benefits.
giving 64 mb to 95

Adding 64 MB of RAM to a computer running Windows 95 would have a significant impact on performance, compared to adding RAM to Windows 1.0-3.0. 

Windows 95 was released in 1995 and it recommended a minimum of 4 MB of RAM, with 8 MB of RAM being the standard for most users. With 64 MB of RAM added to the system, you would have more than enough memory to run multiple programs simultaneously, open large files, and run more demanding applications. It would also make the computer boot up faster, and reduce the need for the operating system to use a paging file (which would slow down the system) when the RAM is insufficient. In summary, adding 64 MB of RAM to a system running Windows 95 would improve its performance significantly.
adding a better cpu as well

Upgrading the CPU in addition to adding 64 MB of RAM to a computer running Windows 95 would likely also result in a noticeable improvement in performance. 

The CPU, or central processing unit, is responsible for executing instructions and performing calculations in the computer. A more advanced processor would be able to handle more instructions per second, making the computer run faster overall.

Windows 95 was designed to run on systems with CPUs ranging from the Intel 80386 to the Pentium line of processors. If you are upgrading an older system, upgrading to a Pentium-class processor can provide a noticeable performance boost. However, to make the upgrade, you will need to make sure that the new CPU is compatible with the computer's motherboard and that it uses the same socket type as the older processor.

Overall, while upgrading the CPU and adding more RAM to a computer running Windows 95 can certainly improve performance, it is worth noting that eventually, there will be hardware and software limitations that the system won't be able to overcome. For example, Windows 95's 16-bit architecture limits its performance and ability to run newer software and operating systems.
how fast does a stock computer.boot 95

The boot time of a stock computer running Windows 95 can vary depending on the specific hardware configuration of the system. However, on average, a computer running a clean installation of Windows 95 on a hard disk drive (HDD) would typically take around 30-60 seconds to boot up.

Keep in mind that this estimate is for the average machine at the time. Modern hardware running virtual machines can potentially boot Windows 95 much faster than the original hardware. However, the actual boot time can vary based on factors such as the speed of the HDD, the amount of memory, the CPU and other hardware, and any software startup items.
brtter cpu and 64 mb of ram

Upgrading a stock computer running Windows 95 with a better CPU and 64 MB of RAM can result in a noticeable improvement in performance, including boot times. The exact boot time will depend on the specific hardware configuration, but as a rough estimate, you can expect the boot time to be around 15-30 seconds with this upgrade.

The faster CPU will be able to execute instructions more quickly and therefore speed up all system processes, including booting up the computer. The additional RAM will allow Windows 95 to expand the size of the page file and access more data more quickly, which can also result in faster boot times.

Keep in mind that boot time is just one aspect of system performance. With these upgrades, you can also expect other tasks such as opening programs, loading files, and running applications to be faster and smoother.
giving 64 mb to 200

It's not clear what you mean by "200". Assuming you are referring to a hypothetical system released in the year 2000, adding 64 MB of RAM to such a system would likely be modest. At the time, typical computers were shipped with 128-256 MB of RAM. 

However, the actual benefit of adding more RAM depends on how the system was used. If the system was primarily used for browsing the web and running basic applications, there might not be a significant performance boost from adding more RAM. However, if the system was used for more demanding applications or tasks such as video editing, photo processing, or gaming, more RAM would definitely help speed things up.

In general, adding more RAM to a system enables it to store more data in memory, which provides faster access to the data when required. This means the system can run more smoothly as it doesn't need to constantly fetch data from the hard disk drive.
